entries.title: 2-class-per-term limit (cross-district only) good to know
entries.body_text: The rule The CVC Exchange limits you to 2 classes per term across districts. If you hit the limit and see a UI error, that's the system enforcing it — not a bug. Important nuance The limit does NOT apply to HC↔HC enrollments (taking a class at another college within your same district). So if you're at one campus of a multi-campus district and want a class at the other campus, the 2-class limit doesn't apply. Need more than 2? Work with your Home College — they can authorize an exception in some cases. Or apply directly to the additional Teaching Colleges outside the Exchange via icangotocollege.com .

entries.title: Federal aid (Pell, FAFSA loans) only disburses at your Home College structural rule
entries.body_text: Why Title IV federal aid is administered at the institution that admitted you — your Home College. The Teaching College places you in a non-Title IV "shell" program for cross-enrollment. Federal aid can't be disbursed there. What this means in practice CVC courses count toward your enrollment status for aid purposes. The aid still comes from your Home College's financial aid office. You pay the Teaching College upfront with a card; the HC reimburses you per your aid package. The TC's FAFSA data is not transferred, and your TC units don't write back to your HC's aid system automatically.

entries.title: Teaching Colleges can auto-drop you for non-payment without telling CVC pay on time, every time
entries.body_text: The risk Each Teaching College has its own policy for dropping unpaid students — and CVC isn't always notified when they do. You may discover you've been dropped only when your class disappears from your dashboard or Canvas. Protect yourself Pay at the moment of registration if possible. If your CVC payment is failing, pay through the Teaching College's portal directly while CVC support investigates. The TC's clock is what matters; their auto-drop is the real risk. If you got dropped Email both CVC and the TC's A&R immediately. Document any payment attempts. A late re-enrollment may be possible if the section is still open and you're within their grace window — but it's case-by-case.

entries.title: You can't change your Home College after enrolling drop + re-enroll required
entries.body_text: Why this is a designed limit The system's verification path links your CCCID to your selected Home College at enrollment time. Re-mapping that mid-stream creates more problems than it solves (financial aid emails, transcripts, MIS reporting all anchor on the original choice). If you got the wrong one Drop the cross-enrolled course → re-cross-enroll with the correct HC → request a refund on the original (refunds take 2–6 weeks). Annoying, but the only path. See §iii.

The local district fees (parking, student services, etc.) are charged by your HC's system but only visible in your HC's portal. Action After registering, log into your Home College's student portal and check the cashier/fees section. Pay any local fees there. They aren't optional.

entries.title: Out-of-state addresses aren't supported in the AER form no workaround
entries.body_text: Why this matters The Authorization to Enroll form requires a California address. This was a deliberate design choice tied to residency verification, but it limits Good Neighbor (border-state) and AB540 students who may not have a CA address on file. If this affects you Talk to your Home College's A&R about updating your address record — some students can update to a CA address used for school correspondence. There's no way to skip this requirement at the CVC level.

entries.title: Once your residency is validated, you can't change it for the term manual fix at HC + TC
entries.body_text: The rule When your Home College validates your residency status at the moment of CVC registration (CA resident or not), that determination is captured in your batch application and persists for the term. The AER form cannot change it after submission. Why each college does its own determination California Title 5 requires every CCC to make its own residency determination for every student it enrolls. There is no shared statewide residency database. Your HC's classification doesn't auto-sync to the Teaching College's SIS — they're separate records. Pre-payment fix If you catch the wrong residency before paying: (1) confirm CA resident status at HC, (2) email TC's A&R with HC proof, (3) once TC updates their record, email CVC support to verify the batch application reflects the new status. Pay only after the rate corrects. Post-payment fix Once payment has cleared, CVC can't update the batch application's residency flag — a designed limit. Whether you get a refund or rebill is the Teaching College's decision (their bursar/student accounts office). Allow 4–6 weeks. See §vi residency guide for the full process.

entries.title: After taking a CVC class at one college in a district, you can't directly register at the sibling college designed guardrail
entries.body_text: What happens Example: a student takes a CVC course at Santiago Canyon College (SCC), then tries to register directly at Santa Ana College (SAC) — both in Rancho Santiago CCD. The TC's portal shows the student as already having a "CVC account" and refuses to let them register through normal admission. Why it's designed this way CCC districts share student records across sibling colleges to avoid double-counting for state funding (FTES). The CVC enrollment path is one record that prevents collisions with separate direct-admission records in the same district. Two paths forward (A) Continue using CVC Exchange for the sibling college — register through CVC selecting the new TC. This works without changes to your record. (B) If you want a direct (non-CVC) student account at the sibling college, contact the district A&R office and request your CVC record be unlinked from the original college. This is a manual process at the district level. Not a CVC support ticket. CVC routes enrollments; the district A&R office decides how their student records are structured. Contact the district directly for the unlinking process.

entries.title: Transcripts back to your Home College aren't always automatic may need follow-up
entries.body_text: What's automatic Grades from your CVC class flow back to your Home College's records system after the term ends — typically around 3 weeks per cvc.edu, though it can vary by college. That part works. What's manual Sending an official transcript to a 4-year college or employer requires the Teaching College to manually trigger an eTranscript send. If you're transferring and need a transcript, request one from the Teaching College — don't assume it's been sent automatically. Each college does this on their schedule.

entries.body_text: Yuba/Woodland-specific Yuba CCD blocks HC↔HC cross-enrollment entirely. If you're at Yuba and want a class at Woodland (or vice versa), you can't use the CVC Exchange for that — apply directly through CCCApply. For other colleges Most allow HC↔HC. Some have quirks (Coast assesses local fees on HC↔HC; Chaffey requires manual indicator monitoring). Check the Teaching College's Confluence Special Handling page if you hit unexpected blocks.

entries.title: stop / fee/CCPG "I was registered then suddenly dropped — Drop Before Class Begins" →
entries.body_text: What happened: some Teaching Colleges automatically drop registrations within 3–5 days if your fees haven't cleared. The "DB" code (Drop Before Class Begins) means the system did this — not you. The most common reason: your California Promise Grant (CCPG) fee waiver hadn't been processed at the Teaching College yet. To match your CCPG to your registration, the Teaching College needs your Social Security Number on file. The CVC enrollment form doesn't collect SSN, so the match has to happen on the Teaching College's side — manually. How to fix it: Email the Teaching College's bursar or financial aid office. Ask them to confirm your SSN is on file so they can process your CCPG. Once that's done , register again through CVC if seats are still open. If the class filled, look for the same course at another Teaching College.

entries.title: "Invalid CCCID" / "CCCID not found" at registration SSO config issue
entries.body_text: What it means Your Home College's login system isn't passing your CCCID through to CVC during single-sign-on. Without the CCCID, the Teaching College has no way to identify you and the registration is rejected. Why it happens Three common causes: (i) you have a CCCID at OpenCCC but your Home College's IT didn't link it in their system; (ii) you have multiple OpenCCC accounts under different emails — the system can't tell which is "you"; (iii) you're a brand-new student whose CCCID hasn't migrated to the HC's records yet. First step (5 minutes) Go to openccc.net , sign in or recover your account, and confirm your CCCID. Verify it matches what your Home College shows on your student portal. If they don't match Contact your Home College's A&R office (not CVC, not Tech Center) and ask them to update your CCCID in their student record. CVC can't update HC records. Real talk — this is the #1 root cause of unsolvable cross-enrollment failures. There's no streamlined fix from the CVC side; it requires the HC to align your record. If your HC isn't responsive, an alternative is to apply directly to the Teaching College via icangotocollege.com while the HC sorts itself out.

entries.title: act / record fix "Could not find the student records at Home College" →
entries.body_text: What's happening: your CCCID was created when you applied to your Home College using a paper application (or another non-standard path), but it never got connected to your actual student record at the college. You may have used CVC before without trouble — but recently your Home College switched on a system that automatically checks records. Now the check fails because the system can't find your CCCID linked to your file. How to fix it: email your Home College's Admissions & Records office and ask them to link your CCCID to your existing student record. The fix has to happen on their side. Once they do it, your cross-enrollment will work normally — no impact on your standing at the college. If your Home College says they can't help, email CVC support. We can reach out to them on your behalf with the technical details.

entries.title: Co-requisite lab error: "prerequisite not met" contact TC A&R
entries.body_text: What it means You're enrolled in the lecture (e.g., ANTH 101), but the system blocks the companion lab (ANTH 101L) saying you don't meet the prerequisite — even though the lab is a co-requisite, not a prerequisite. The check fires before recognizing your concurrent enrollment. Why it happens The Teaching College's SIS evaluates the lab as a standalone course, not as paired with the lecture. The instructor and TC A&R can manually clear it. First step Email the lab instructor explaining you're already in the lecture. Loop in the Teaching College's A&R office. They typically clear the override within 1–2 business days. CVC support's role Limited — this is a TC-side override. CVC can escalate but the actual unlock happens at the TC. Action — email the TC instructor + A&R first. CVC support second if no response within 2 business days.

entries.title: My prereq application was denied even though I qualify re-review path
entries.body_text: First — read the denial reason Check your CVC inbox for the denial email. It usually states one of: missing documentation, insufficient prereq evidence, class already started, or system error during waiver. Different reasons need different fixes. If "missing documentation" or "insufficient prereq" Re-submit with stronger evidence: official transcript (not unofficial), a letter from the prereq instructor confirming you've completed the equivalent, or your HC's assessment results. Email these directly to the Teaching College's A&R office along with your re-review request. If "class already started" CVC stops accepting registrations after the TC's add deadline (typically the day classes begin). If your AER was submitted before but the prereq review took past the start date, the TC may still accept a late add — you'll need to apply directly to the TC and use the instructor's add code. If "system error" — prereq waiver wasn't applied This is a CVC bug worth reporting. Email CVC support with: CCCID, course, X-tracing ID (in your CVC inbox), and the exact denial text. We can re-trigger the prereq waiver and re-submit your AER. Don't simply re-submit a fresh AER. A second submission while the first is still in the system creates duplicate batch applications. Resolve the original denial through the TC or CVC support before re-applying.

entries.title: Drop Failed email — once
entries.body_text: Most likely cause A hold on your Teaching College account. By far the most common reason — the TC's SIS refuses to drop a student with an active hold (often financial, sometimes registration). Banner-based colleges (Saddleback, College of the Mountains/CMC, others) hit this pattern frequently. Other causes Drop-without-W deadline already passed at the TC, OR a real system / middleware error. Each has a different fix path. Don't retry Repeated attempts create duplicate records that slow resolution. One careful try, then stop. Time pressure If your drop-without-W deadline is within 72 hours, mark the email URGENT and follow the parallel-path advice. What works fastest — (i) check your Teaching College account for any hold (financial, registration, or other) and clear it directly with the TC's A&R office; (ii) email CVC support once with your CCCID, course/CRN, an error screenshot, and the deadline date — the team can request a manual drop on the back end if needed; (iii) email the TC's A&R in parallel for documented timely intent. Use the ticket builder .

entries.title: Login error: "duplicate account" or password reset loop try logout + cookies
entries.body_text: What it means You have multiple OpenCCC profiles (often from old applications under different emails or schools), and the SSO sees them as duplicates — blocks login or sends you in a password-reset loop. Try this first (90 seconds) (i) Log out of all OpenCCC sessions globally — there's a "log out everywhere" link on OpenCCC. (ii) Clear browser cookies for openccc.net and your CCC's SSO domain. (iii) Try a different browser, or incognito/private mode. (iv) Confirm your CCCID is correctly linked at your Home College's portal. If still broken This is a CCC Tech Center issue, not a CVC issue. Email cccsupport@openccc.net with: your full name, both emails involved (if known), date of birth, and a screenshot. They can merge or retire the duplicate profile. Why CVC can't fix it CVC doesn't have admin access to OpenCCC profiles. The CCC Tech Center owns the identity layer. Don't email CVC first — try the 4-step browser fix, then go to CCC Tech Center if it persists. CVC routes you to them anyway.

entries.title: A past payment was declined and I just found out documentation request
entries.body_text: What this is A scenario that surfaces months later: the Teaching College contacts you to say they didn't receive payment, and CVC's records show the original payment was declined — but you weren't notified at the time. Rare but real. What CVC can provide Documentation of the declined transaction: timestamp, last-4 of the card, amount. Useful for verifying with your bank and for re-submitting payment if needed. Email what you have CCCID, the term/course in question, what the Teaching College told you, and the approximate date of the original attempt. CVC can pull the transaction record from their side. Realistic outcome If the decline is documented, you can resubmit the payment — sometimes with help from the TC if the term is closed. Email support with your transaction details — they'll provide documentation you can use with the TC and your bank.

entries.title: Prerequisite Pending Review wait
entries.body_text: What it means Teaching College's A&R is reviewing your prerequisite documents. You are not denied — you are waiting. Whose ball The Teaching College's A&R office. Timeline Typically around 3–5 business days for CCC prereqs; usually a bit longer (around 5–7) for non-CCC documents (private, four-year, out-of-state). Each Teaching College sets its own pace. When to email After the timeline above, or if class starts within 48 hours. Critical — do not resubmit while waiting. Duplicate submissions create conflicting records and slow the review. One application at a time.

entries.title: Prereq still pending and class has already started act now — escalate to TC
entries.body_text: What it means You submitted prerequisite docs in advance, but the Teaching College's A&R hasn't reviewed them yet — and your class has now started. Different from "Prerequisite Pending Review" alone (which is normal); the urgency comes from the class actually being in session. Why this happens Non-CCC prereqs (private, 4-year, out-of-state) need manual review; some TCs are slower than others, especially smaller or rural colleges. Once class starts, every day you wait you fall further behind. First step (today) Email the Teaching College's A&R office directly — don't wait on CVC. Subject: "URGENT — class started [date], prereq pending review for [course] / CCCID [yours]." Attach the same prereq docs you submitted to CVC. CVC's role here Can also escalate, but the TC's A&R is the one who actually approves. Going directly cuts a step. Don't passively wait — once class is in session, reach out to the TC every business day until resolved. Your tone can be polite + persistent. Document each contact.

entries.title: stop / known gap "My prereq was approved but the class is now full" →
entries.body_text: What happened: while the Teaching College was reviewing your prerequisite, the class filled. CVC doesn't hold a seat for you during prereq review — so when your prereq finally got approved, there was no seat left, and the registration failed. This is a known gap in the platform. Your prereq was approved — someone else just got the seat first. There's no waitlist on the Exchange. What to do, in order of how quickly each works: Find another open section of the same class at a different Teaching College — go to search.cvc.edu and search again. Other colleges often have seats. Apply directly to the Teaching College outside CVC, at icangotocollege.com . They may have their own waitlist or late-add process. Take the class next term. Tip for next time: when you submit a prereq for a class that fills fast, find a backup section right away. If the first one fills while you wait, you can register the backup without losing days.

entries.title: Transcript on hold for "Records Research" verify name spelling
entries.body_text: What it means Parchment received your transcript request but couldn't match it to a record at the Teaching College. Almost always a name mismatch — the name you used when ordering doesn't exactly match what the TC has on file. Common cases Last name with two parts (e.g., "Pereyra Palacio" vs "Pereyra"); preferred vs legal name; hyphenated names; accents/diacritics omitted; first/middle initial added or dropped. First step Look up exactly what name the Teaching College has on file (usually visible in their student portal or on a prior transcript). Re-submit the Parchment order with that exact name spelling. If still stuck Email Parchment's CVC support (cvc.customersupport@parchment.com) with your order confirmation number, the email used, your CCCID, and the exact name as it appears at the Teaching College.

entries.title: Registration Failed (no specific message) check detail
entries.body_text: What it means "Registration Failed" is a category, not a specific cause. The actual reason lives in the Application Errors field on your enrollment record. Where to look CVC dashboard → Class Schedule → click the failed enrollment → read the error message exactly. Action — match the exact error text against this decoder. If it mentions "Course Filled," "prerequisite," "residency," or "hold," see the matching entry. If it's something else, copy the exact text and email support.

entries.title: My Home College fixed my residency but the Teaching College still charges me out-of-state two-college fix
entries.body_text: Why this happens Residency lives in each college's own SIS — there is no single statewide residency record. When your HC reclassifies you, that update stays at your HC unless you also push it to the TC. CVC reads what the TC has on file at the moment you registered, which is why your tuition was set then. If you haven't paid yet Email the Teaching College's A&R office. Subject: "Residency reclassification — please update for CVC enrollment." Include your TC student ID, the course name, the exact rate you're being charged, and proof of your CA residency at your HC (screenshot or HC A&R letter). The TC updates their record; tuition recalculates. Then email CVC support to confirm the batch application reflects the new status before you pay. If you've already paid the out-of-state rate CVC can't update the batch application's residency flag once payment has cleared — that's a designed limit. Whether you get a refund or rebill is the Teaching College's decision: contact their bursar/student accounts office and request a residency reclassification refund. Allow 4–6 weeks. Some TCs rebill automatically; others require a written request. What CVC can do — verify the data flow, confirm what the TC has on file, and (pre-payment only) update the batch application's residency flag once the TC confirms. What CVC can't do — override the TC's residency determination, issue refunds, or change a paid batch application. See §vi residency guide for the full process.

entries.title: "Live Seat Count: Unavailable" — can't tell if seats are open try anyway
entries.body_text: What it means CVC's seat-count display can fall back to "Unavailable" when the Teaching College's SIS isn't returning live seat data — usually a temporary integration issue, not a sold-out class. Has been seen as a cluster at some TCs (Coalinga, Lemoore, Peralta-area colleges) tied to specific SIS configurations. What to do Try the registration anyway — the seat check happens server-side at submit, so an "Unavailable" display doesn't actually block the attempt. If your AER goes through and reaches "Pending Validation," seats were available. If it fails with "Course Filled," they weren't. If it fails repeatedly Email CVC support with CCCID, course, TC, exact timestamp, and the unavailable display. We verify with the TC's A&R whether seats actually exist and report any cluster patterns to the implementation tracker. Don't wait. The display issue doesn't gate registration. Each minute you wait, real seats may be claimed by someone else who tried.

entries.title: act / decode each "I see two errors stacked — am I in big trouble?" →
entries.body_text: Don't keep clicking submit. Each rapid retry creates extra error messages on top of the real one — that makes it harder to figure out what's actually wrong. Wait at least 5–10 minutes between tries, or stop and read this entry first. When you click "register" several times in a row, the system often shows two errors instead of one. Here's how they usually work: The real error — the actual reason your registration failed (prereq, section full, unit overload, hold, etc.). This is the one that matters. The leftover error — usually says "Another registration session is in progress for this student ID and term. Please try again later." This isn't a second problem; it's just a side effect of clicking too fast. How to tell them apart: the real error names something specific (a course code, a unit count, a hold reason). The leftover error is generic and mentions "session in progress." Fix the real one — the leftover disappears on its own once the system releases its lock (usually 5–10 minutes). What to do: wait 10 minutes for the lock to clear. Then look at the first registration error you saw — not the most recent one. That first one tells you what's actually wrong. If you can't find it anymore, check the Application Errors field on the failed registration in your dashboard — it's saved there.

entries.title: stop / TC decision "Petition is required" / "puts student in overload" →
entries.body_text: What's happening: the Teaching College has a limit on how many units you can take in one term, and adding this class would put you over the limit. Common limits: Summer: usually 7 units max Fall / Spring: usually 18 units max Winter intersession: usually 6 units max CVC can't raise the limit. You have two options: Drop one of your other classes so your total fits under the cap, then register for the new one. Ask the Teaching College for a unit-overload petition. Their counseling office or A&R reviews these and decides. Petitions can take days, so if the seat is filling fast, weigh the timing.

entries.title: "System can't verify my community college account" — Canvas email mismatch two-path fix
entries.body_text: What's actually happening (despite what the error says) The error message is misleading. CVC has already confirmed you as a community college student — that's not the problem. The mismatch is in Canvas (the learning system where your class actually meets). Our system tries to match the email on your CVC profile to an email on your Canvas account at your Home College, and it can't find one. Until those line up, the bridge between CVC and Canvas can't link your enrollment. Two ways to fix it — easiest first Option 1 — update your CVC profile email to match your Canvas email at your HC. Log into your CVC account at search.cvc.edu . Go to Profile . Change the email to whatever address you use in Canvas at your Home College (typically your school .edu address). Save and retry your enrollment. Option 2 — add your CVC profile email to Canvas at your HC. Log into your HC's Canvas (URL varies by district — common patterns: [district].instructure.com ). Go to Profile → Settings . Add your CVC profile email (the personal one), verify it via the confirmation email Canvas sends, and set it as your default . Retry your enrollment. If neither path works A few less-common causes can produce the same error: (i) you've been admitted to a CCC but haven't actually enrolled in a class yet — admission alone isn't enough; (ii) you have multiple OpenCCC accounts under different emails, so the system has multiple CCCIDs for you (see duplicate-login ); (iii) your Home College isn't yet a live CVC partner (check the colleges list ). If you've never applied to a CCC at all CVC isn't a college you apply to — it routes between CCCs you're already enrolled at. Apply first via icangotocollege.com . Once you're admitted and enrolled in at least one class at your Home College, return to CVC. See the first-time setup guide . What CVC support can and can't do — we can confirm what your CVC profile shows and what your enrollment record looks like on our side. We can't update Canvas identity at your Home College — that's their Canvas admin / IT. If both fix paths above don't resolve it, email CVC support with your CCCID, your HC, and the exact error text; if the bridge looks right on our side, we'll route you to the right office at your HC.

entries.title: "Add Class" appears but registration fails — and it's evening wait until midnight PST
entries.body_text: What it means There's a quirk in the timing of when the registration window opens. The "Add Class" button appears at 4 PM PST the day before open enrollment — but registrations submitted between 4 PM PST and midnight PST will silently fail . The system is technically open at midnight in the system's clock, not at 4 PM in yours. When this hits you You're trying to register the evening before classes open at your Teaching College. You see "Add Class," click it, fill out the form — and get a generic error or stuck state. The fix Wait until after midnight Pacific Time on the open-enrollment date and try again. Don't keep retrying in the meantime — the failed attempts can leave artifacts that complicate the next try. When to email support If you tried after midnight PST and it still fails. Otherwise this resolves itself in a few hours. Tell your friends — this gotcha is real and unfixed. If you know the open date, register Day 1 morning, not Day 0 evening.

entries.title: I selected the wrong Home College at signup drop + re-enroll
entries.body_text: What this is When you first cross-enrolled, you picked a Home College where you have a login (maybe an old account) but it's not where you currently attend. The cross-enrollment goes through, but downstream things break: financial aid emails go to the wrong place, transcripts may route to the wrong institution, MIS reporting at the TC may be off. Can it be changed? No — manually changing the Home College after the fact is not supported. The system's verification and authentication paths weren't designed for that, and a back-end change creates more problems than it solves. The fix Drop the cross-enrolled course and re-enroll, this time selecting the correct Home College. Yes, you'll need to pay again and wait for a refund on the original. Yes, this is annoying. There's no shortcut. Already finished the class? Email CVC support — late-stage HC corrections are rare and case-by-case. They may need to escalate internally. Don't keep cross-enrolling under the wrong HC hoping it sorts itself out. It won't. The longer the wrong HC is on your record, the messier downstream gets.

entries.title: Will my CVC class count for full-time status or financial aid? no — talk to your Home College
entries.body_text: Short answer No , not for federal aid purposes. Your Home College only counts the units you're taking at the Home College when figuring out your Pell. CVC units don't add to that total — there's no agreement in place that combines them. In practice If you're taking 3 units at your Home College and 3 units through CVC, your Home College sees 3 units — not 6. That's the same way they calculate full-time status and SAP (the rules about staying on track for aid). Important step before the term starts If you were counting on your CVC class to get you to a higher tier of Pell, full-time status, or to keep your SAP on track — check with your Home College's financial aid office now . Don't wait until you've already started the term.

entries.title: Can I use my Pell Grant at the Teaching College? no — by design
entries.body_text: Short answer No. The Teaching College isn't set up to handle federal aid (the technical term is "not Title IV eligible"). Your Pell stays at your Home College. What this means for your Pell amount Your Home College figures out your Pell based on the units you take at the Home College only . They don't add your CVC units to the total. So if you're taking 3 units at your Home College and 3 units through CVC, your Pell is calculated on 3 units — not 6. CVC doesn't combine units across colleges for federal aid. If you were counting on a higher Pell amount Talk to your Home College's financial aid office before the term starts . Don't assume your CVC units will bump up your tier or count toward full-time status. Better to know now than be surprised later. What about my CCPG fee waiver? CCPG is different from Pell — it can apply at the Teaching College. But for the match to work, the Teaching College needs your Social Security Number on file. CVC's enrollment form doesn't collect SSN, so the Teaching College has to add it manually. Email their Admissions & Records or Financial Aid office and ask them to confirm your SSN is on file.

entries.title: stop / eligibility "I'm a new student at my Home College — can I use CVC yet?" →
entries.body_text: Short answer: not until you've registered for at least one class at your Home College. California law (AB-637) says you can only use CVC if you've "completed a class or are currently taking a class" at your Home College. If you just applied and haven't enrolled in anything yet, you're not eligible yet — the system blocks you on purpose. What you'll see if you try anyway "Canvas account not found with your email" — this is the system catching it. Your Home College's Canvas hasn't created an account for you because you're not in any classes there yet. "Ineligible" status on your CVC dashboard. Your Home College telling you they "can't give you a Canvas account" — that's normal. Canvas is created when you register for a class, not when you apply. What to do: Register for one class at your Home College for the current term. If you haven't applied yet, do that first at icangotocollege.com . If you've already applied, log into your Home College's student portal and register for a class. Once you're enrolled there and Canvas has set up your account, come back to CVC — it'll work.

entries.title: Tuition shows $400+ for 3 units don't pay
entries.body_text: What it means Your California residency wasn't confirmed at the Teaching College , so the system is charging you out-of-state tuition. Each college keeps its own residency record — your Home College's record doesn't automatically transfer. In-state rate $46 per unit. $138 for 3 units. $184 for 4. Real ticket examples we've seen of out-of-state pricing: $1,000 (3 units), $1,400 (3 units), $1,864 (4 units). If your number is anywhere near 3–9× the in-state rate, it's a residency issue — not a bug. The 2-step fix (1) Confirm your residency is up to date at your Home College's A&R office. (2) Send proof of that residency status (a screenshot of your HC portal showing "California Resident," or a letter from HC's A&R) to the Teaching College's A&R office. The TC must update their own residency flag — neither CVC nor your HC can do it for them. Special cases AB540 — file with the TC separately (not just HC). Active-duty military — your HC's Veterans Resource Center sends a Parent School Authorization Letter to the TC. Recently reclassified — see the full residency guide below. Do not pay until tuition recalculates. If you've already paid the out-of-state rate and your residency is later corrected, the Teaching College decides whether to rebill or refund — CVC can't issue refunds. Read the full §vi residency guide for the email templates and special-population paths.

entries.title: Get a 1098-T tax form for a CVC class Each Teaching College issues its own — CVC and your HC don't have it
entries.body_text: Tax matters — verify with the source. CVC routes course enrollment data; we don't issue tax forms or give tax advice. For your specific 1098-T, contact the Teaching College's Bursar / Student Accounts office directly — they have the authoritative record. For tax interpretation, consult a tax professional or the IRS. Information below is general and may not match your specific situation. The IRS Form 1098-T is the tuition statement colleges send to students who paid qualifying education expenses. For a CVC course, the Teaching College that taught the class is the college that issues the 1098-T — not CVC, not your Home College. This trips students up every January when they go looking for the form on their HC's portal and don't see it. Where to find it Identify which Teaching College taught the class. Your CVC dashboard's Class Schedule shows the TC for each course you've taken. Contact that TC's Bursar / Student Accounts office. Tell them you took a CVC Exchange class with them in [year] and need a 1098-T. They'll either point you to a self-service portal, mail it, or email it. Repeat for every TC you used. If you took CVC classes at three different Teaching Colleges, you'll receive three separate 1098-Ts. CVC does not consolidate them. Common confusion — students assume their Home College's 1098-T includes the CVC class because that's where their financial aid disbursed. It doesn't. The TC issues a 1098-T because the TC received your tuition payment. Each TC = each form. If you can't reach the TC's portal You may not have a TC student login (CVC doesn't normally provision one). Email the TC's Bursar / Student Accounts office directly with: your full name, your CCCID, the term and course you took, and the email and mailing address you want the form sent to. Most TCs accommodate this. Note: this entry is informational only. For tax advice, talk to a tax professional — CVC support, your HC, and your TC's bursar can confirm what they sent and where, but none of us can advise on whether a specific expense is deductible.

entries.title: First time using CVC? Here's what each account does Three accounts work together — get them aligned, or verification fails
entries.body_text: If verification is failing right now — see "System can't verify my community college account" . This guide is the foundational explainer. The most common confusion at first sign-up is thinking CVC Exchange is itself a college you apply to. It's not. CVC routes you between CCCs you're already enrolled at , which means three separate accounts have to line up before CVC can verify you. When a student tells us "the system can't find my college account," it's almost always because one of these three is missing or out of sync with the others. The three accounts OpenCCC account — your statewide California Community Colleges identity. This is where your CCCID (the 7-character ID like CAF3028) lives. One per person, ever. If you've ever applied to any CCC, you have one. Found at opencccapply.net . Home College student account — your record at the CCC you primarily attend. This is where you log in to your HC's portal, where your transcripts live, where your degree is issued, where financial aid disburses. Created when you applied via CCCApply and were admitted to that college. Your HC almost always assigns you a school-issued email (typically a .edu address) when you enroll. CVC Exchange profile — your account on cvc.edu. It doesn't exist independently — it pulls from OpenCCC at every login and looks up your enrollment at the HC. If your OpenCCC record doesn't match your HC's record, CVC has nothing to verify against and shows you as not enrolled. Why the three have to align CVC's verification step asks: "Does this OpenCCC profile (with this CCCID) have an active enrollment at one of our partner CCCs?" To answer "yes," the data has to be: Same person — your name, DOB, and SSN (last 4) on OpenCCC must match the same fields on your HC student record. Same CCCID — your HC ties their internal student record to your CCCID. Common breakage: students create a second OpenCCC account under a different email (different CCCID) and try to log in with that one. Active enrollment — admission alone isn't enough. You have to be currently enrolled in a class at your HC (or have completed one). Email mismatches — the most common cause Your OpenCCC email and your HC student email can be different — that's allowed. But: Many HCs use SSO (single sign-on) for CVC login, which authenticates against your school-issued .edu email . If you signed up at OpenCCC with your personal email (Gmail, Yahoo) and try to log into CVC via your HC's SSO, the system may not bridge the two records correctly. If you used a personal (non-edu) email at OpenCCC, CVC may default to "I can't verify your community college enrollment" because the SSO bridge from your HC didn't recognize the email. Fix: update your OpenCCC email to match what your HC has on file (typically your school's .edu address). Update at opencccapply.net → My Account → Personal Information. Allow 24–48 hours for CVC to resync. If you don't know what email your HC has on file, log into your HC's student portal — your profile page typically shows the email of record. Or contact your HC's A&R office. If you've never applied to a CCC before You can't use CVC Exchange. Apply to a community college first via icangotocollege.com (the CCCApply portal). Once admitted and enrolled in at least one class, your HC's record will be in the system — then return to CVC and your verification will succeed. Quick checklist before you panic Are you currently enrolled in a class at a California Community College? (Admission alone isn't enough.) Is the email on your OpenCCC profile the same one your HC has? (If unsure: HC portal → Profile.) Do you have only one OpenCCC account ? (Multiple = multiple CCCIDs = login chaos.) Is your HC active on CVC Exchange (not just listed)? See the colleges list on cvc.edu. If all four are yes and CVC still can't verify, see err-verify-account for the diagnostic and fix path.

entries.title: Use financial aid (CCPG, FAFSA, Pell) CVC works as reimbursement, not direct pay
entries.body_text: You (student) Home College where aid lives Teaching College where you pay first ① pay upfront (card) ② aid reimburses (if approved) Money flows two ways. You pay the Teaching College upfront. Your Home College reimburses you per your aid package — only if you're enrolled in 6+ units at your HC and your request is approved. Federal aid never disburses at the Teaching College. Always check with your Home College's Financial Aid office for the most accurate, up-to-date guidance on your specific situation. The information below is general — your aid eligibility, packaging, and timing depend on factors only your HC's FA staff can confirm. Read this first — per CVC's official guidance: "Clicking the request box does not mean you will receive financial aid." Submitting a financial aid request during enrollment only sends the request to your Home College for review. You are still responsible for paying the Teaching College upfront while your Home College considers your request. If you don't pay, the Teaching College drops you — even if your aid is later approved. How payment actually works You pay the Teaching College upfront with a card. Your Home College reimburses you according to your aid package, after the fact. Aid does not get sent ahead to the Teaching College on your behalf — it always disburses through your Home College only. Federal aid eligibility — the 6-unit rule Per cvc.edu: "You must be registered in at least six units at your home college to be eligible" for federal financial aid consideration on CVC courses. CVC class units alone don't count for federal aid — the threshold is what you carry at the HC. CCPG (California College Promise Grant) CCPG can cover per-unit fees on a CVC course, but the application happens at the Teaching College , not your HC. The official process from cvc.edu: Visit your Exchange Profile → Class Schedule Click Pay Now next to your course Choose "California Promise Grant (CCPG)" from the payment options Complete the Teaching College's financial aid application SSN script — the CVC enrollment form doesn't collect SSN. For CCPG to match your award at the Teaching College, they need your SSN on file. If your CCPG isn't applying, contact the Teaching College's A&R or Financial Aid office and ask them to confirm your SSN is on record. That's the fix — not a CVC bug. Pell Grant Pell can't be disbursed at the Teaching College. CVC students sit in a non-Title IV "shell" program at the Teaching College. Pell still works at your Home College for classes you take there. CVC courses count toward your enrollment status for aid purposes, but the disbursement is at the Home College. Veterans benefits Veterans can use VA benefits on CVC courses. The process: your Home College's Veterans Resource Center handles benefit certification and contacts the Teaching College with a Parent School Authorization Letter . Talk to your HC's VRC before registering so the paperwork is in place. Full info: cvc.edu/students/financial-aid

entries.title: My class was cancelled by the Teaching College Refunds come from the TC's bursar, not CVC
entries.body_text: Sometimes a Teaching College cancels a section before it starts (low enrollment, instructor unavailability). Your CVC dashboard may or may not catch up immediately. The refund process runs through the TC, not CVC. Steps Confirm the cancellation with the Teaching College's A&R office. Get the cancellation in writing (email or formal notice) — you'll need it for the refund. Contact the TC's Bursar / Student Accounts office with that confirmation. State that you paid through CVC for [course] in [term] and the section was cancelled. Ask for a refund processed back to your original payment method. Allow 4–6 weeks. Email CVC support in parallel — we can update your batch application's status to "cancelled" so the dashboard reflects reality. We can't issue the refund (only the TC can), but we can confirm to the TC that you paid through us. If you used financial aid at the TC (CCPG, etc.), that part of the refund may flow back to the aid program rather than to you. The TC's financial aid office handles that side. Different from a self-drop refund — when YOU drop a class within the refund period, that's a different process. See §vi residency for non-resident refund issues, or the cvc.edu refunds page for general drop refunds. Cancelled-by-TC is its own path.

entries.title: Drop or switch a class Order matters — and money doesn't transfer
entries.body_text: Just dropping i. Log in to search.cvc.edu . ii. Class Schedule → click Drop Course on the class. iii. Confirm. Don't repeat-click if the page seems slow. If you get "Drop Failed" — the most common cause is an account hold at the Teaching College (often financial). Check the TC's student portal for any holds, clear them with the TC's A&R or cashier, then try the drop again. See §iii. Drop Failed for the full diagnosis. Switching classes i. Register for the new class first. This locks the seat. If it fills before you drop the old one, you've lost both. ii. Pay for the new class. iii. Drop the old class. iv. Request a refund from the Teaching College of the dropped class. Find the process at cvc.edu/students/refunds . Refunds take 2–6 weeks. Important — Money does not transfer between classes. You'll pay separately for the new class and wait for the refund from the dropped one. Find your drop deadline Drop deadlines are per-section, not per-term. Two classes in the same semester can have different deadlines. Look at the section details on your CVC dashboard. Or check the Teaching College's website — most publish a deadline-by-CRN tool. Or email the Teaching College's A&R office. Two deadlines to watch: Last Day to Drop — No Notation (clean drop, no transcript record), and Last Day to Drop with "W" (drop later, get a W on transcript).

entries.title: Eligibility — special cases High school, F-1, AB540, Adult Ed, holds
entries.body_text: Need ALL of these to be eligible ✓ 18 years or older ✓ Currently enrolled at a CCC Home College (or actively taking a class there if it's your first term) ✓ 2.0 GPA or no established GPA yet ✓ No outstanding fees at your Home College ✓ California address on file at your Home College NOT eligible to cross-enroll via CVC × Dual-enrolled high school students — apply directly to the Teaching College via CCCApply × F-1 visa students — talk to your DSO about your options × Adult Education students — typically not permitted; check with your AE program Source: cvc.edu/students/student-eligibility — the authoritative source. For any planning question — talk to your Home College's academic counselor first. They know your degree path, prerequisites, transfer plan, and special-admit status better than any FAQ can. The list below covers eligibility rules ; whether a specific class fits your plan is counselor territory. The basic requirements (all must be true) You are at least 18 years old. You are currently enrolled at a CCC Home College. If this is your first term at your Home College — or you were recently admitted — you must either have completed a class there, or be currently taking a class that's in progress. Acceptance alone isn't enough. You have at least a 2.0 GPA at your Home College — or you have not yet established a GPA (first-term students with no completed classes). You have no outstanding fees at your Home College. You have an in-state (California) address on file at your Home College. Populations NOT eligible to cross-enroll via CVC Dual-enrolled high school students — cvc.edu states explicitly: "Dual-enrolled high school students are not eligible to enroll in courses via the CVC Exchange, due to the unique policies and procedures in place." If you're an HS dual-enrollment student, talk to your Home College's special-admit counselor or apply directly to the Teaching College. International students on F-1 visa — federal visa regulations restrict concurrent enrollment outside your sponsoring school. Per cvc.edu: F-1 students are "not able to enroll in courses via the CVC Exchange." Talk to your Designated School Official (DSO) about your options. Adult Education students — typically not permitted via the Exchange. Contact your Adult Ed program coordinator. Special cases that ARE eligible (with extra steps) AB540 (California Nonresident Tuition Exemption): Eligible. First establish AB540 status with your Home College. For CCPG, apply at the Teaching College through CCCApply or the California Dream Act Application with the TC's CADAA code. Veterans: Eligible. Your Home College's Veterans Resource Center handles benefit certification and contacts the Teaching College with a Parent School Authorization Letter. Talk to your HC's VRC before registering. Returning adult / non-traditional students: Eligible if you meet the basic requirements above (CCC enrollment, 18+, 2.0 GPA or none yet, no fees, CA address). Other things that block enrollment Hold on Home College account: clear it first. The system blocks new registrations while a hold is active. Low GPA at Home College: system off-ramps you. Resolve the GPA flag at HC before retrying. Home College not on Exchange: apply directly to the Teaching College at icangotocollege.com . Full rules: cvc.edu/students/student-eligibility (the authoritative source)

entries.title: Change the email on your CVC account CVC syncs from OpenCCC — update there, not on CVC directly
entries.body_text: Your CVC Exchange account doesn't have its own editable email field — it pulls from your OpenCCC profile . Updating the email on CVC alone won't stick; the next sync overwrites it. The fix is to change it at OpenCCC. Steps Go to opencccapply.net and sign in with your existing OpenCCC credentials. Open My Account → Personal Information . Update the email address. Save. Allow 24–48 hours for the change to propagate to CVC. The next time you log into search.cvc.edu, your new email should reflect. If your OpenCCC email is what you sign in with (not just contact), update your sign-in email at OpenCCC's account settings — that's a separate field. Edge case — if you can't sign in to OpenCCC because you've lost access to the email tied to it, contact OpenCCC support directly (not CVC). They handle account recovery. If your Home College changed email systems on you Some HCs migrate from one email provider to another (e.g., from Google Workspace to Microsoft) and can break login flows for ex-students. If the change locked you out, contact your HC's IT helpdesk first — they can issue a new credential. Then update OpenCCC and CVC will resync.

entries.title: What success looks like at each step Match your dashboard against this — if yours matches, you're fine
entries.body_text: 1 Created you started 2 Consent signed form 3 AER Submitted enrollment form 4 Eligible & Approved ~minutes 5 Pending Validation ~1–2 biz days 6 Validated & Registered you're in 7 Enrolled & Paid click Confirm! 8 Canvas access ~24–48h — You — — System review — — Take the class — The full registration journey. Steps 1–3 are you. Steps 4–6 are CVC and the Teaching College reviewing. Step 7 is paying. Step 8 is Canvas opening up. Times shown are typical, but each Teaching College sets its own pace. The whole journey from first click to "you're enrolled" runs through these states. Each is normal. If you're stuck on one for longer than expected, that's where to start asking. i. Created — you started a CVC enrollment. Just means you got into the form. ii. Consent Given — you signed the consent form (typed your full name). iii. AER Form Submitted — you completed the enrollment form and uploaded any prereqs. iv. Eligible and Approved — system check passed. CVC handed off to the Teaching College. Sometimes shows "Skipped home and teaching approvals" — that means a counselor cleared you via multiple-measures placement. Both are normal. If stuck here past 5 business days: see Pending Person Match . v. Registered & Pending Validation — Teaching College added you to their SIS. Final automated check running. vi. Validated & Registered — you're in. Pay if you haven't. vii. Enrolled & Paid — payment confirmed. Wait 24–48 hours for Canvas access. viii. Take the class — Canvas opens, instructor activates content, term runs. ix. Term ends — Teaching College submits grades. Allow 4–6 weeks for them to flow back to your Home College's transcript. x. Transferring later? Order transcripts from each college individually . CVC doesn't consolidate. Diagnostic shortcut — if your status today matches step vi or higher, you're enrolled. Stop worrying. Pay if needed and check Canvas in 24–48h.

entries.title: I paid the Teaching College directly (outside CVC's payment flow) Possible to reconcile, but the dashboard won't auto-update
entries.body_text: Sometimes CVC's payment page fails repeatedly (TouchNet glitches, Cybersource timeouts, the TC's processor down) and the only way to actually pay is to go directly to the Teaching College's portal or call their cashier. This works to keep your enrollment alive — but CVC's dashboard won't know about the payment until someone tells it. If you've already paid the TC directly Email CVC support with: CCCID, course, term, the date and method of your direct payment, and the receipt/reference number from the TC. We verify with the Teaching College's bursar that the payment cleared. Once confirmed, we update your batch application's payment status — your dashboard will reflect "Enrolled & Paid" within 1–2 business days. You don't need to do anything more at the TC's end once payment is confirmed. If you're considering paying directly — pause first Direct payment is a workaround, not a recommended path. Try once more through CVC's flow (different browser, different card, after-hours retry) before going around it. Direct payment can create reconciliation issues if you miss a step. If CVC's payment page repeatedly fails: Email CVC support describing the exact error message you see. Ask if the TC's payment processor is having a known outage (TouchNet has had multi-hour outages). Wait an hour and retry through CVC. Only go direct if the deadline is imminent and CVC's flow keeps failing.

entries.title: Find your past CVC enrollments (and their transcripts) Especially if you took CCC Online classes years ago and need them now
entries.body_text: Old records vary — contact each college's A&R. CVC's current Exchange platform may not have records older than 2020, and CCC Online's archival data lives at each individual college. For an authoritative transcript or enrollment record, contact the Teaching College's Admissions & Records office directly. They keep the academic record; CVC just routed the enrollment. For transfer planning around old credits, your Home College's academic counselor is the right office. This comes up most often when students apply to graduate programs (PA school, nursing, transfer) and need transcripts from every college they've ever taken classes at — including CVC Exchange courses they may not remember the details of. The good news: every record exists. The not-so-good: it's in pieces. Where your past CVC records live Your CVC Exchange dashboard at search.cvc.edu shows your enrollment history within the current Exchange platform — typically going back to the launch of CVC's current system. Each Teaching College keeps the academic record (grades, transcripts) for the class you took with them. They issue the official transcript, not CVC. Older "CCC Online" enrollments (the predecessor to CVC Exchange) may not appear in the current dashboard. Those records are at the Teaching Colleges that hosted the courses — but you may need to remember which colleges those were. If your dashboard is missing past enrollments Check your Home College's transcript first. Per CVC's official process, classes you took via Exchange were automatically transcripted back to your HC (typically within 3 weeks of term end). Your HC transcript should list every CVC class as transfer credit, including the Teaching College and term. Check your Canvas history. Log into your HC's Canvas and click "All Courses." Past CVC courses should appear in the archive — even if just as a course shell. The course code and term will tell you which TC. Email CVC support with: your CCCID, your name on file, approximate years/terms you took CVC courses. We can look up your historical batch applications and tell you which TCs you used. Then contact each TC's A&R to order an official transcript. (CVC does not order transcripts on your behalf — see §vi transcripts .) If you applied for older CCC Online classes via FERPA-protected paper records — note that some CCCs purge inactive student records after a fixed retention period (often 5 years). If a TC's A&R can't find your record, ask if they have a long-term archive or if it was purged.

entries.title: Pay tuition The Confirm Payment trap and how to avoid it
entries.body_text: i. From your CVC dashboard, click Pay Fees on the course. ii. You'll be redirected to the Teaching College's payment page (often TouchNet, NelNet, or Cybersource). Enter your card. iii. After the bank approves, you're redirected back to a CVC page with a Confirm Payment button. Don't close the browser yet. If you do, your card is charged but the Teaching College won't see you as paid. They may drop you. Click Confirm Payment. iv. Verify the dashboard now says Enrolled & Paid. If it still says Pay Now, the confirmation didn't register — see the error decoder.

entries.title: What is the CVC Exchange? (60-second primer) Read this if you're new — orientation to home/teaching college, cost, journey
entries.body_text: Home College where you log in, where aid lives Teaching College where the class actually meets CVC Exchange enrollment · payment grades · transcripts Two colleges, one routing layer. CVC sits between them; enrollment and payment flow from you to the Teaching College, and grades/transcripts flow back to your Home College. The California Virtual Campus (CVC) Exchange is a system run by the California Community Colleges that lets you take a class at a different CCC than the one you primarily attend — without a separate application. Think of it as a routing layer between 115 community colleges. The two-college concept Home College — the CCC you primarily attend. Where you log in from. Where your degree or certificate is issued. Where all financial aid disburses (Pell, FAFSA, CCPG). The college whose name appears on your final award. Teaching College — the CCC offering the specific class you want. Where the class meets. Where you pay for that class. Where the transcript of that class lives until you order it sent. What CVC handles vs what your colleges handle CVC Exchange handles: routing your registration, payment processing, the data passing between colleges. Your Home College handles: aid disbursement, your degree/certificate, your overall record, counseling and articulation. Your Teaching College handles: the actual class, grading, the transcript of that class, drop deadlines, refunds. What it costs In-state CCC tuition is $46/unit for California residents. A 3-unit class is $138; a 4-unit class is $184. Out-of-state students pay much more. If your tuition shows $400+ for 3 units, your residency wasn't confirmed at the Teaching College — see the tuition entry . Who's eligible Concurrently enrolled at a CCC (Home College), 18 years old, 2.0 GPA (or no established GPA), no outstanding fees, and a California address on file. Special populations (high school, F-1, AB540, Adult Ed) need extra paperwork — see §vi eligibility .

entries.title: Register for a class 5 steps with the prereq + payment gotchas inline
entries.body_text: i. Search at search.cvc.edu and click Add Class on the section you want. ii. Log in through your Home College (the one you already attend). Gotcha — if your Home College isn't listed, you can't cross-enroll. Apply directly to the Teaching College at icangotocollege.com instead. iii. Type your full name on the consent form to sign it. iv. Fill the enrollment form. Upload prerequisite documents if asked. Confirm CA residency. Gotcha — non-CCC prereqs (4-year, private, out-of-state) need manual review and take 5–7 business days, not 3. v. Click Submit, then pay immediately. Always click Confirm Payment after the bank step — this is the single biggest cause of payment tickets. Before you submit a registration ticket I've waited at least 3 business days since registering (5+ for non-CCC prereqs). I've checked my CVC inbox for any document requests from the Teaching College. I haven't resubmitted while the original was pending. I've read the exact error message (not just "Failed").

entries.title: Repeat a course (e.g., for nursing recency requirement) Petition path — your Home College's counselor leads, CVC routes
entries.body_text: Some programs (nursing being the most common) have a "recency requirement" — a course taken more than 5 or 7 years ago doesn't count toward admission. If you've already taken the equivalent course at a CCC and need to retake it, the standard "no repeat for credit" rule needs a petition. Steps Talk to your Home College's academic counselor first. They handle academic petitions and know your degree path. They'll review whether the recency rule actually requires a repeat or if there's another way (challenge exam, recent equivalent). If repeat is the right path , your HC counselor files a course-repeat petition with HC's Articulation/Academic Standards office. Get an approval letter showing the petition is granted. Find a Teaching College offering the same C-ID equivalent course via CVC search. The course at the TC must articulate to the same C-ID as your HC course for the petition to apply. Cross-enroll through CVC normally. Upload the HC's repeat petition approval as part of your prerequisite documentation when prompted, or email it to the TC's A&R office immediately after submitting the AER. Counselor-led decision — recency rules vary by university and program. Your counselor is the one who knows whether the schools you're applying to (UC, CSU, private) accept repeats and under what conditions. CVC routes the cross-enrollment but cannot determine whether a repeat satisfies your specific transfer plan.

entries.title: Fix California residency for CVC tuition Why your HC's "fix" doesn't sync to the Teaching College — and how to actually correct it
entries.body_text: Talk to both colleges' A&R offices first. Residency determinations are made by each college separately under California Title 5 — CVC support can verify the data flow but cannot override either college's determination. The advice below covers the process ; whether a specific document satisfies a specific college's residency rules is up to their A&R staff. This is one of the top reasons students email support. The frustration is real: you're a California resident, you've lived here for years, your Home College already has you classified as in-state — and yet your CVC tuition shows out-of-state pricing of $1,000+ for a single class. The reason is structural, not a bug, and the fix is a specific 2-step process. Why each college has its own residency record Under California Education Code, every community college is required to make its own residency determination for every student it enrolls — including cross-enrollment students. There is no shared statewide residency database. Your Home College's "California Resident" classification lives in their SIS (Banner, Colleague, or PeopleSoft); the Teaching College's classification lives in their SIS. CVC reads what the TC has on file at the moment you submit the AER form, and that becomes the basis for tuition. So when your residency status is wrong on a CVC course, the question isn't "did my HC fix it" — it's "did the Teaching College update their record." The 2-step fix (most students) Confirm your residency at your Home College. Log into your HC's portal and verify your residency classification reads "California Resident" (or your HC's equivalent label). If it doesn't, contact your HC's A&R office with proof: utility bill, CA driver's license, lease/mortgage, voter registration, CA tax return — your HC can tell you which documents they need. Push that classification to the Teaching College. Email the TC's A&R office with: (a) your TC student ID, (b) the CVC course you're enrolled in, (c) the rate you're being charged, and (d) proof of HC residency — a screenshot of your HC portal showing the CA Resident status, or a letter from your HC A&R. Ask them to update your residency classification in their system. Their A&R is who fixes it — not their financial aid office, not CVC, not your HC. Once the TC updates their record, your tuition recalculates to the in-state rate. Email CVC support if you want us to verify the batch application reflects the correct status before you pay. Special case — AB540 (California Nonresident Tuition Exemption) AB540 grants in-state tuition rates to certain undocumented and other qualifying students who attended a CA high school and meet specific criteria. AB540 status is determined by each college separately , just like regular residency. Process: File the California Nonresident Tuition Exemption Request (CNTER) form at your Home College. They review and approve. File the same CNTER form again at the Teaching College . Their A&R reviews and approves separately. Some TCs accept a copy of the HC's approval letter as part of the documentation; some require fresh documentation. Once the TC approves AB540, your CVC tuition recalculates to in-state. For fee waiver coverage: apply for CCPG via California Dream Act Application (CADAA) using the Teaching College's CADAA school code. CCPG funding flows through whichever college you applied at. Special case — Active-duty military Active-duty service members stationed in California (and their dependents) qualify for in-state tuition under California Education Code §68075 regardless of formal residency. Process: Provide your military orders or LES (Leave and Earnings Statement) to your Home College's Veterans Resource Center (VRC) . They certify your military status. The HC's VRC sends a Parent School Authorization Letter to the Teaching College with confirmation of your in-state military status. (You may need to request the PSA explicitly — call the HC's VRC and ask for it by name.) The TC processes the PSA and updates their residency flag to in-state for tuition purposes. Don't wait until after registration — talk to your HC's VRC before you register through CVC so the PSA is in place when the AER form is submitted. Special case — Veterans benefits (post-service) If you're using VA benefits (GI Bill, etc.) for a CVC course, your HC's VRC handles benefit certification and sends a Parent School Authorization Letter to the Teaching College. The benefits flow through your HC; the PSA tells the TC you're cleared. Talk to your HC's VRC before registering. Special case — recently reclassified (HC fixed it after you paid) If you registered while your HC had you classified as non-resident, paid the out-of-state tuition, then later got reclassified to CA resident — refunding the overpayment is the Teaching College's call, not CVC's. Some Teaching Colleges: Rebill automatically when their A&R updates the residency flag retroactively (favorable but not universal). Require a written request to the bursar/student accounts office — usually a one-page form or email referencing the term, course, and updated residency status. Decline retroactive refunds citing a "frozen at registration" policy. Rare but it happens. Allow 4–6 weeks for any TC-side refund process. CVC's batch application can't be retroactively updated once payment has cleared — that's a designed limit (see §ix lim-residency ). Special case — you're genuinely an out-of-state resident If you live outside California and are charged the non-resident rate, the system is working as designed. CVC Exchange does not waive non-resident tuition for purely out-of-state students. Possible paths if applicable: (a) AB540 if you meet the high-school-attendance criteria, (b) military exemption if active duty, (c) wait until you've established California residency through your HC (typically a year of physical presence + intent — your HC's A&R will tell you what counts). Email template — Teaching College A&R Subject: Residency reclassification request — [your TC student ID] Dear [Teaching College] Admissions & Records, I'm a California resident enrolled at [Home College name] and recently registered for [course name and CRN] at your college through the CVC Exchange. My account is showing the non-resident tuition rate ([dollar amount]), but I am classified as a California Resident at my Home College. Attached: (a) screenshot of my [Home College] portal showing my California Resident classification, (b) a copy of my [CA driver's license / utility bill / lease — whatever your HC accepted]. Could you please update my residency classification in your system so the in-state tuition rate ($46/unit) applies? I want to confirm the rate is correct before I pay. Thank you, [your name] / [your TC student ID] / [CVC Exchange registration date] What CVC support can and can't do Can: verify what the Teaching College has on file, confirm what your batch application captured at registration, update the batch application's residency flag pre-payment once the TC confirms the change, route you to the right office. Can't: override the Teaching College's residency determination, change a residency flag after payment has cleared, issue refunds (refunds always come from the Teaching College's bursar, not CVC), influence Title 5 residency policy. Common mistakes to avoid Paying first, fixing later. Once you've paid the non-resident rate, you're at the TC's discretion for a refund. Always verify the rate is correct before clicking Pay. Assuming the HC fix syncs to the TC. It doesn't. Each college maintains its own record. The 2-step fix is required. Asking your HC to update the TC's record. Your HC has no access to the TC's SIS. Only the TC's A&R can change the TC's residency flag. Asking CVC to issue a refund. CVC routes the data; refunds and rebills are the Teaching College's bursar. Source: cvc.edu/students/student-residency · CA Education Code §68075 (military residency) · Title 5 §54000+ (residency determination)

entries.title: Use the search filters effectively Find the right course in 30 seconds — by transfer requirement, format, cost, or schedule
entries.body_text: Most students miss half the search filters at cvc.edu . Knowing what each one does saves time: Search by Keyword — search course titles + descriptions Subject — biology, English, math, etc. Home College Course Name (via C-ID mapping) — find a class equivalent to one your HC offers CSU GE-Breadth — courses that count toward CSU transfer GE IGETC — courses that count toward UC transfer GE Filter by Instant Enrollment — sections that allow immediate registration (no waiting for a TC review) Available Seats — only sections with seats right now (live count) Online Format — synchronous (live class meeting times) vs asynchronous (work on your own schedule) Course Prerequisites — toggle whether to show classes with prereqs Term, Term Start/End Dates — narrow to a specific session Zero Textbook Cost (ZTC) — sections marked as having no required textbook fees Transferability — courses that transfer to UC, CSU, or other systems Teaching College — restrict to a specific college Number of Units — minimum and maximum unit counts Heads-up on seat counts — live seat counts are real-time, but the underlying course catalog imports overnight. A class showing as available can fill up between viewing and registering. If you find one you want, register quickly. Pro tip — if you're transfer-bound, filter by IGETC (for UC) or CSU GE-Breadth (for CSU) and you'll only see classes that count toward your transfer requirements. Saves you from taking units that don't apply.

entries.title: When does the summer / fall / spring schedule appear? Each Teaching College sets its own publish date — and the registration window is separate
entries.body_text: Term-specific dates — verify with each college. CVC doesn't set the academic calendar. Each Teaching College publishes its own schedule and registration window on its own timeline. For confirmed dates, check the Teaching College's Admissions & Records page or academic calendar; for advising on which term fits your transfer plan, talk to your Home College's academic counselor . The cadences below are typical patterns — not guarantees. "When can I register?" is one of the most common pre-registration questions. The answer is more nuanced than a single date — there are two distinct events, set independently by each Teaching College. Two different dates to track Schedule visibility — when the TC publishes their term catalog, making courses searchable on cvc.edu. Typically around 60–90 days before the term starts , but each TC sets its own date. Some publish all at once; some roll out by department. Registration window opens — when the "Add Class" button activates for cross-enrollment. Often the same day as schedule visibility, but not always — some TCs publish the catalog first as a preview, then open registration a week or two later. Typical CCC term cadence (varies) Summer term — schedules typically appear March–April; registration opens shortly after. Fall term — schedules typically appear April–May; registration opens by June for most TCs. Spring term — schedules typically appear October–November; registration opens by December. These windows are typical but not guaranteed. Each TC publishes on their own academic calendar — check the specific TC's website for confirmed dates. The 4 PM PST gotcha Even when the TC announces a registration open date, CVC's "Add Class" button appears at 4 PM PST the day before . But registrations submitted between 4 PM and midnight PST will silently fail. Wait until midnight Pacific Time on the announced open date. Full err-window details . Pre-registration checklist While you wait for visibility, make sure your account is ready: Confirmed enrolled at your Home College for the upcoming term (or actively taking a class if first-term). No outstanding holds at your HC. Residency confirmed at HC (so the AER form pulls correct data). If you're a special population (AB540, military, veterans), paperwork already filed at HC. Don't email asking when courses will be visible. CVC support can't accelerate a TC's catalog publish. Bookmark cvc.edu and check periodically; most browsers can set a watch on a specific search URL.

entries.title: Get a transcript Your home college vs. external schools
entries.body_text: Teaching College Home College automatic automatic — around 3 weeks 4-year college or employer you order it — TC's request process Two transcript paths after your class. The grade flows back to your Home College automatically. Sending an official transcript to a 4-year college or employer requires you to order it from the Teaching College through their normal request process. Sending grades back to your Home College Automatic. Per cvc.edu: "the college teaching the course will automatically send an electronic copy of your transcript to your Home College" after the term ends. Timeline is typically around 3 weeks , but it varies by college and term. If grades aren't visible after that, contact your Home College's A&R first — they often just need to refresh. Note: if your CVC class was taken to fulfill a prerequisite, credit may not appear automatically on your HC transcript. Initiate a transcript evaluation with your HC's counselor or evaluations office. Sending transcripts to a 4-year college or employer Request from the Teaching College that taught the course — not from your Home College, not from CVC. Most colleges use Parchment; some have other providers. You'll need your Teaching College ID number, which is on your CVC Exchange profile at search.cvc.edu/user/edit . Important when you transfer — per CVC's official guidance: "when you are ready to transfer, you will still need to order transcripts from each college individually." If you took three CVC classes at three different Teaching Colleges, that's three separate transcript orders, plus one from your Home College. CVC does not consolidate them. Details: cvc.edu/students/transcripts-students
